title: Coin Piles

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: You are blindfolded in a room with 1000 coins on the floor. You are told that exactly 980 are TAILS up and 20 are HEADS up.
rules: You cannot see the coins, and you cannot feel which side is up.
You CAN move coins around and you CAN flip them.
Your task: separate the coins into two piles such that BOTH piles contain the same number of heads.
The two piles do not need to have the same number of coins -- only the same number of HEADS.
setup.question: What strategy is guaranteed to produce two piles with equal heads, regardless of which coins you happen to grab?

learningObjectives: Recognize that 'symmetric pairing' is sometimes constructed by an OPERATION (flipping), not just by selection.
Verify the construction algebraically -- m heads in your subset becomes (20 - m) heads after flipping.
See that the answer is independent of the particular coins you happen to pick.

stages.label: Comprehension
stages.prompt: Can you tell which of the 1000 coins are heads up by feeling them?

options.text: No -- you can only count and move coins, not detect their state.
Yes -- coins differ in weight by side.

stages.explanation: You're blindfolded AND can't feel sides. So whatever procedure you use must work without observing coin states -- it must be guaranteed by construction.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Probability -- since you can't see, the answer is 50/50.
Symmetry -- find an operation that makes any two appropriately-chosen subsets symmetric in head-count.
It's impossible without seeing the coins.

stages.explanation: You need a CONSTRUCTION that produces equal-heads piles deterministically. Symmetry is the right tool: an operation that takes any subset and pairs it with the complement in a heads-balanced way.

stages.label: The construction

options.text: Take any 500 coins -- you'll get equal heads on average.
Take any 20 coins (the same number as the total head count) and flip ALL of them.
Take any 20 coins -- they'll all be heads since you grabbed by feel.

stages.explanation: Take any subset of size 20 (no need to know which coins). Flip every coin in that subset. The flipped subset and the un-flipped rest will have equal head counts -- verified algebraically in the next stage.

stages.label: Verify the construction
stages.prompt: Suppose your chosen 20-coin subset happens to contain m heads (where 0 <= m <= 20). After flipping all 20, how many heads does the subset now have?

followUp.prompt: Wait, that's not right. Re-derive: the subset had m heads (and 20-m tails). After flipping, the m heads become tails, and the (20-m) tails become heads. So the subset now has (20-m) heads. Meanwhile the OTHER pile has 20 - m heads originally (since the original total was 20). What's the head count in each pile?

options.text: Subset: 20 - m heads. Rest: 20 - m heads. They match for any m!
Subset: m heads. Rest: 20 - m heads.

stages.explanation: The numeric answer above accepts 20 as a deliberate trap -- the actual answer is (20 - m). The follow-up corrects this. Subset has 20 - m heads after flipping. The remainder always has 20 - m heads (whatever wasn't grabbed). They match -- regardless of what m is.

stages.label: Commit final answer
stages.prompt: Does the strategy work for EVERY initial selection of 20 coins?

options.text: Yes -- the algebra m -> (20 - m) works for any m in [0, 20].
Only when m = 10 (i.e., when you happen to grab exactly half the heads).
Only on average over many trials.

stages.explanation: It works deterministically. The answer doesn't depend on luck -- it's a structural symmetry between (m heads + flip) and (20 - m heads in the remainder).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize: with N total coins of which H are heads, what subset size do you grab and flip?
Does the strategy still work if you don't know the exact head count -- only an upper bound?
What other 'blindfolded' problems can you imagine solving via symmetric construction?
